component_identification.content: Core box includes 1 Wavelength console with rotating target wheel and clear scoring window, 1 magnetic needle dial, 2 team scoring markers, 1 track board integrated in the console, 126 double-sided spectrum cards (252 prompts), and rulebook. Components are shared by both teams; no hidden hand of cards exists. Some editions include quick-reference cards.

core_game_loop.content: Turn rhythm: active side reveals or frames information, teammates/opponents respond under that game’s communication limits, then resolve points or survival state and rotate responsibility. Teach this as a repeating three-step cycle: present signal, interpret signal, then resolve consequences. New players should always know whose decision matters right now and what hidden information cannot be shared. Most confusion comes from breaking communication restrictions or skipping reveal order, so keep sequence strict.

detailed_rules.content: Split into two teams. Secretly set target on hidden spectrum. Active clue-giver sees exact target location, then gives a one-part clue that helps teammates place the needle along the printed spectrum axis (e.g., Hot–Cold, Bad Habit–Good Habit). Team discusses and locks needle position. Reveal target; score based on distance (bullseye gives max points, near miss gives fewer). Opposing team usually gets a chance to guess which side of your needle the true target lies on for steal/bonus points depending on edition rules. Pass clue-giver role and alternate teams. Key restrictions: clue-giver cannot use numbers, coordinate-like hints, or multi-clue chaining that bypasses intuition game; once final lock is made, do not adjust after reveal starts; resolve steals only after primary points are awarded. End when a team reaches target score on track.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Each round awards points based on needle distance to hidden target (center bullseye highest, adjacent bands lower). Opponents may gain bonus/steal points for correct side guess in standard team rules. Game ends when a team marker reaches final score space (commonly 10). If both can reach in same round, resolve by official tie procedure (usually higher total after full round).

beginner_strategy.content: 1) Give clues that imply relative placement, not exact numeric thought. 2) Teammates should calibrate clue-giver personality over rounds; same clue can map differently by person. 3) Opponent side-guessing is high leverage—listen for overconfident locks. 4) Avoid meta clues that break spirit; consistency beats clever loopholes. 5) In close games, prioritize reliable medium scores over risky bullseye hunts.

metadata.notes: Check 4 failed: total_token_count is 484; must be 800-3000 (hard reject if <800 or >5000)
